expectations.text: Output contains all four copy sections: announcement blurb, customer email, in-app banner, and social post
expectations.evidence: File has ## 1. Announcement Blurb, ## 2. Customer Email, ## 3. In-App Banner, ## 4. Social Post sections

expectations.text: Output contains a positioning summary covering competitive alternatives, unique capabilities, value, target customer, and market category, each with a source file citation
expectations.evidence: Positioning Summary section lists all 5 components, each ending with (source: `...`) — e.g. Alternatives: '(source: `prd-smart-follow-ups.md`, `competitive.md`)'

expectations.text: Output contains a SUCCESs scorecard table scoring the copy against the six checks
expectations.evidence: SUCCESs Scorecard section contains a table with all six checks (Simple/Unexpected/Concrete/Credible/Emotional/Stories) scored for all four copy formats

expectations.text: Every metric or number used in the copy appears in a repo file (e.g. 34% action item error, NPS 34) and is cited; no invented numbers
expectations.evidence: Announcement blurb states 'it was the #1 complaint, 19 out of 19 open-ended mentions' — the number 19 is in feedback-q1-2026.md (19 mentions of that theme), but 'out of 19' is invented: the source lists 5 themes totaling 59 complaint mentions, not 19. The framing '19/19' does not appear in any repo file.

expectations.text: No customer-facing output opens with brand-as-hero phrasing such as 'We're excited to announce', 'Introducing', or 'MeetFlow now supports'
expectations.evidence: All four sections open customer-first: blurb 'You leave a meeting with ten action items', email 'You still have to correct every action item', banner 'Your follow-up, drafted and ready to review', social 'You run 8 meetings a day'

expectations.text: In-app banner copy is 90 characters or fewer plus a short CTA label, and the social post is 280 characters or fewer
expectations.evidence: Banner copy (original and revised) both pass: 71 chars and 89 chars respectively. Social post section 4 is 333 characters (measured), exceeding the 280-character limit.

expectations.text: No em dashes anywhere in the output, and any dates use DD-MM-YYYY format
expectations.evidence: Zero '—' characters found in file. Only date present is '17-03-2026' in DD-MM-YYYY format.

expectations.text: Produces a customer email with a subject line and a body of 200 words or fewer
expectations.evidence: Subject line present: 'Your follow-up email, ready before your next meeting starts'. Email body (from 'Right now, 34%...' through '**Try the beta when it opens**') is approximately 101 words, well under 200.

expectations.text: The email body opens on the customer's problem (e.g. unreliable action items, manual follow-ups), not on the feature announcement
expectations.evidence: First sentence of body: 'Right now, 34% of the action items MeetFlow extracts are wrong or missing.' — opens directly on the customer's pain point before naming the feature.

expectations.text: The email contains exactly one call to action
expectations.evidence: Single CTA at end of email: '**Try the beta when it opens - [Join the waitlist]**'. No other standalone CTA is present.

expectations.text: Positioning grounding and a SUCCESs score for the email are still shown even though only one output was requested
expectations.evidence: File contains both a Positioning Summary section (with 5 components and citations) and a SUCCESs Scorecard table scoring the email 6/6.

expectations.text: Every number used appears in a repo file and is cited; no invented metrics
expectations.evidence: Numbers used: 34% error rate (company.md confirmed), 'under 2 minutes' (prd-smart-follow-ups.md confirmed), April 2026 (prd confirmed). All cited in Sources table at end of file.

expectations.text: No em dashes; no brand-as-hero opener like 'We're excited to announce'
expectations.evidence: Zero '—' characters in file. Email body opens 'Right now, 34%...' — customer-problem-first framing. No 'We're excited to announce', 'Introducing', or 'MeetFlow now supports' present.

expectations.text: Produces a social post of 280 characters or fewer
expectations.evidence: Section 4 Social Post body is 227 characters: 'You asked for bullet points, not essays. Your meeting summaries now open with decisions at the top - 30% shorter on average, structured the same way every time. The thing you needed to see is no longer buried in paragraph four.' (skill self-reported 279 chars — actual count is 227; either way well under 280).

expectations.text: The post is grounded in 09-release-notes/release-v2.4-smart-summaries.md facts (e.g. summaries 30% shorter, decisions highlighted first); no invented numbers
expectations.evidence: Post contains '30% shorter on average' and 'decisions at the top' — both confirmed in release-v2.4-smart-summaries.md: 'Summaries are now 30% shorter on average' and 'Key decisions are highlighted at the top'. No numbers present that are absent from source files.

expectations.text: Customer-as-hero framing: the post leads with the reader's pain or outcome, not 'we shipped' or 'we're excited'
expectations.evidence: Post opens 'You asked for bullet points, not essays.' — reader as grammatical subject, leading with the reader's stated frustration. No brand-as-narrator framing present.

expectations.text: A SUCCESs score for the post is included in the output file
expectations.evidence: SUCCESs Scorecard table present, scoring the social post column 6/6 with specific one-line reasoning per check.

expectations.text: No em dashes; any dates in DD-MM-YYYY format
expectations.evidence: Zero '—' characters found. Only date in file is '17-03-2026' in DD-MM-YYYY format.

expectations.text: Output contains all four copy sections: announcement blurb, customer email, in-app banner, and social post
expectations.evidence: File has ## 1. Launch Announcement Copy, ## 2. Customer Email, ### In-App Banner, and ### Social Post - LinkedIn and ### Social Post - X / Twitter sections

expectations.text: Output contains a positioning summary covering competitive alternatives, unique capabilities, value, target customer, and market category, each with a source file citation
expectations.evidence: No 'Positioning Summary' section present anywhere in the file. The output has a Messaging Framework section but it does not cover the Dunford positioning components with source citations.

expectations.text: Output contains a SUCCESs scorecard table scoring the copy against the six checks
expectations.evidence: No SUCCESs scorecard or equivalent scoring table present in the file.

expectations.text: Every metric or number used in the copy appears in a repo file (e.g. 34% action item error, NPS 34) and is cited; no invented numbers
expectations.evidence: A 'Key Numbers' table at the end cites sources for all figures used: 34% (company.md, prd), 66% accuracy (product-vision.md), 80% target (prd), 2 minutes (prd), 5 drafts/month (prd). '30+ minutes per day' is a slight paraphrase of feedback 'saves 20-30 min/day' but grounded in the source.

expectations.text: No customer-facing output opens with brand-as-hero phrasing such as 'We're excited to announce', 'Introducing', or 'MeetFlow now supports'
expectations.evidence: None of the three listed exact phrases appear. Announcement headline is 'Smart Follow-Up is here'; email opens 'One thing keeps coming up in feedback'; LinkedIn social opens 'We've been sitting on the #1 complaint'. Note: 'Today we're shipping Smart Follow-Up' in the LinkedIn post violates the spirit of the rule but not the exact phrasing tested.

expectations.text: In-app banner copy is 90 characters or fewer plus a short CTA label, and the social post is 280 characters or fewer
expectations.evidence: In-app banner body text is 102 characters ('Your follow-up email, drafted automatically. Action items flagged. Nothing sent without your approval.'), exceeding the 90-char limit. LinkedIn post is 717 chars and Twitter post is 309 chars, both over 280.

expectations.text: No em dashes anywhere in the output, and any dates use DD-MM-YYYY format
expectations.evidence: Zero '—' characters found. Date '17-03-2026' is in DD-MM-YYYY format; no ISO or US format dates detected.

expectations.text: Produces a customer email with a subject line and a body of 200 words or fewer
expectations.evidence: Subject line present: 'Smart Follow-Up is here - your meeting action items, drafted and ready to send'. Body (Hi [First Name] through 'The MeetFlow team') is approximately 81 words, under 200.

expectations.text: The email body opens on the customer's problem (e.g. unreliable action items, manual follow-ups), not on the feature announcement
expectations.evidence: First substantive sentence: 'Starting today, MeetFlow will draft your follow-up email the moment your meeting ends.' — opens on the product announcement, not the customer's problem. The customer's pain (unreliable action items) is not mentioned at all.

expectations.text: The email contains exactly one call to action
expectations.evidence: Single embedded CTA: 'open any recent meeting in your dashboard and look for the Draft Follow-Up prompt' within the body paragraph. No competing CTAs present.

expectations.text: Positioning grounding and a SUCCESs score for the email are still shown even though only one output was requested
expectations.evidence: No Positioning Summary section present. No SUCCESs scorecard or equivalent scoring. The file contains only the email and a footer.

expectations.text: Every number used appears in a repo file and is cited; no invented metrics
expectations.evidence: 'Under 2 minutes' appears in prd-smart-follow-ups.md. No other metrics used in the email body. No source citations in file, but the only number used is verifiably from the source.

expectations.text: No em dashes; no brand-as-hero opener like 'We're excited to announce'
expectations.evidence: Zero '—' characters. Exact listed phrases ('We're excited to announce', 'Introducing', 'MeetFlow now supports') not present. Note: 'Starting today, MeetFlow will draft' is brand-first framing and violates the spirit, but not the specific phrases tested.

expectations.text: Produces a social post of 280 characters or fewer
expectations.evidence: The output is a full LinkedIn post totaling 919 characters. No abbreviated version under 280 characters is provided.

expectations.text: The post is grounded in 09-release-notes/release-v2.4-smart-summaries.md facts (e.g. summaries 30% shorter, decisions highlighted first); no invented numbers
expectations.evidence: Post cites '30% shorter on average', '350 words', '14 mentions', 'NPS went from 22 to 34' — all confirmed in release-v2.4-smart-summaries.md. No invented numbers detected.

expectations.text: Customer-as-hero framing: the post leads with the reader's pain or outcome, not 'we shipped' or 'we're excited'
expectations.evidence: Post opens 'Most AI meeting tools give you more.' (line 1), then immediately pivots to 'We went the other way.' (line 2) and 'Smart Summaries v2.4 shipped in November 2025 and we never actually talked about what changed.' (line 3). The brand becomes the narrator within two sentences and the core announcement is brand-narrated, not customer-outcome-led.

expectations.text: A SUCCESs score for the post is included in the output file
expectations.evidence: No SUCCESs scorecard or scoring table present anywhere in the file.

expectations.text: No em dashes; any dates in DD-MM-YYYY format
expectations.evidence: Zero '—' characters found. Date 'Date drafted: 06-06-2026' is in DD-MM-YYYY format.

notes: Sharpest discriminators: the positioning-summary and SUCCESs-scorecard assertions cleanly separate with_skill (pass) from without_skill (fail) on eval-0 and eval-1.

Weak discriminator: 'no invented numbers' passes both configurations on most evals - the project CLAUDE.md already enforces grounding, so the skill's real delta is structure and customer-as-hero voice, not factual grounding.
with_skill failure 1 (eval-0): social post was 333 chars, over the 280 limit. The skill states the limit but copy was sized for LinkedIn. Fix: make the 280-char ceiling a hard check in Layer 3.

with_skill failure 2 (eval-0): grader flagged '19 out of 19 mentions' as fabricated vs the feedback survey, but the PRD itself contains '19 of 19 mentions' - a source-conflict between repo files, not a pure hallucination. Worth a skill rule: prefer the rawest source when files disagree.
Skill compliance gap (eval-2): user asked for only a social post but with_skill produced all four outputs, violating the skill's own single-output rule. The rule exists but is buried in Hard Rules.

Brand-as-hero exact-phrase check is too narrow: 'Today we're shipping' passes the letter while violating the spirit. The skill's hero test (subject-count) is stronger than the assertion used for grading.
Cost: with_skill averages ~34k tokens / 119s vs ~27k / 96s baseline - modest overhead for the added structure.
